front: What is bus arbitration and why is it necessary in a computer system?
back: Bus arbitration is the process of determining which bus master will gain control of the bus when multiple devices request it simultaneously. It is necessary to prevent conflicts and ensure orderly communication when more than one device can initiate data transfers.

front: What are the two main categories of bus arbitration techniques?
back: The two main categories are centralized arbitration (using a single arbiter) and distributed arbitration (where control logic is shared among all potential masters).

front: Describe the Daisy-Chain arbitration method, including its key advantage and disadvantage.
back: A centralized, priority-based method where a bus grant signal is passed serially through devices. The device closest to the arbiter has the highest priority. Advantage: Simple and requires few control lines. Disadvant: Fixed priority can cause starvation for low-priority devices and has a variable arbitration time.
The central arbiter sequentially polls each device to see if it needs the bus. Devices are serviced in a fixed or rotating order (round-robin), determined by a polling sequence stored in a register.

front: What is the key principle behind Independent Request (Parallel Priority) arbitration?
back: Each bus master has its own dedicated pair of bus request and bus grant lines connected directly to the central arbiter. The arbiter uses a priority algorithm (e.g., fixed priority, round-robin) to decide which request to grant, and it can make the decision in a fixed time.
